Wearable gait sensors predict observable but not unobservable motor severity in Parkinson's disease

[Author names to be added]

[Affiliations to be added]

Abstract

The MDS-UPDRS Part III motor examination is the clinical gold standard for Parkinson's disease (PD) severity assessment, yet its 18 items span motor functions ranging from gait and posture—directly manifest during ambulation—to rigidity, speech, and facial expression, which require clinical examination modalities that no body-worn sensor can capture. Here we present the first UPDRS-III regression benchmark on WearGait-PD, the largest controlled-gait dataset with complete motor scores (N = 178 subjects, 98 PD, 80 healthy controls, 13 IMU sensors at 100 Hz). In PD-only leave-one-out cross-validation (N = 98), our foundation-model-augmented pipeline achieves MAE = 8.15 (CCC = 0.37, r = 0.429) for total UPDRS-III—notably, a demographic Ridge baseline using age, sex, disease duration, height, and weight achieves MAE = 7.86, outperforming all sensor models on this composite endpoint. Nevertheless, partial correlation r = 0.36 (padj = 0.002) after controlling for age and disease duration confirms that the inertial signal contributes beyond demographic confounds. Critically, decomposing the score into three observability tiers reveals a structural prediction ceiling: directly observable items (3.9–3.14: gait, posture, arising, body bradykinesia) achieve CCC = 0.56 and MAE = 1.77 (N = 94), while partially observable (CCC = 0.12) and clinically unobservable items (CCC = 0.18) remain poorly predicted. (Note: the MCID of 3.25 was derived for longitudinal change detection2; comparison with cross-sectional prediction error is contextual.) Feature–anatomy alignment validates the mechanism: foot and trunk sensors drive gait-item predictions, while wrist sensors drive upper-limb items. Seven deep learning configurations across three architecture families, MiniRocket kernels, and multiple feature engineering strategies all converge on the same total-score ceiling, suggesting that the barrier is determined by item observability rather than modeling methodology. We propose that the directly observable motor subdomain is a more appropriate endpoint for wearable-based PD monitoring than the total composite score.

1. Introduction

Parkinson's disease (PD) affects over 8.5 million people worldwide, making it the fastest-growing neurological disorder1. The Movement Disorder Society Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale Part III (MDS-UPDRS-III) is the gold standard for motor severity assessment, comprising 18 items (33 sub-items) scored 0–4 each (total range 0–132). Administration requires a trained clinician, takes 20–30 minutes, and is inherently subjective—inter-rater variability can exceed the minimally clinically important difference (MCID) of 3.25 points2.

Body-worn inertial measurement units (IMUs) offer a path toward continuous, objective motor monitoring. Several groups have attempted UPDRS-III regression from wearable sensors. Hssayeni et al. achieved MAE = 5.95 with an ensemble of three deep learning models on 24 PD patients using wrist and ankle gyroscopes during free-living activities (LOOCV)3. Shuqair et al. improved correlation to r = 0.89 on the same 24-patient dataset using self-supervised pretraining4. Both studies used leave-one-out cross-validation on small, PD-only cohorts, limiting generalizability. Other reported results suffer from methodological concerns: the IS22 result (MAE = 4.26) contained confirmed window-level data leakage5, and the TRIP benchmark on WearGait-PD addressed only classification, not regression6.

WearGait-PD is the largest publicly available controlled-gait dataset with complete MDS-UPDRS-III scores, comprising 185 enrolled subjects (100 PD, 85 HC), of whom 178 (98 PD, 80 HC) had complete recordings7. No published UPDRS-III regression exists on this dataset.

We present four contributions. First, we establish a rigorous regression benchmark on WearGait-PD with subject-level evaluation protocols including PD-only LOOCV, multi-split cross-validation, and a pre-registered held-out test. Second, we decompose UPDRS-III into three observability tiers—directly observable, partially observable, and clinically unobservable from gait—revealing a structural prediction ceiling that explains why total-score regression plateaus regardless of modeling sophistication. Third, we demonstrate that frozen foundation model embeddings from MOMENT-18 significantly outperform handcrafted features in mixed-cohort evaluation (p = 0.0020), though this advantage diminishes in PD-only evaluation where within-disease severity discrimination is harder than PD-vs-HC separation. Fourth, we show that a 5-sensor minimal set achieves equivalent performance to the full 13-sensor configuration (p = 0.85), informing clinical deployment.

2. Results

2.1 Cohort Description

Of 185 enrolled participants, 178 (98 PD, 80 HC) had complete sensor recordings and were included (Table 1). PD participants (mean age 67.0 ± 8.3 years, 65M/35F) had moderate motor severity (UPDRS-III 24.2 ± 10.9, range [0.0, 59.0]). Hoehn & Yahr staging was available for 95 patients (mean 2.15 ± 0.6). The HC group was older (74.1 ± 9.2 years, p < 0.001) with lower motor scores (7.1 ± 9.6). Medication state (ON/OFF) was not systematically controlled in the WearGait-PD protocol.

2.2 Total UPDRS-III Prediction: The Demographic Baseline Challenge

In PD-only 10-split cross-validation (N = 98, Table 2), our best IMU model (LightGBM with handcrafted features, PD-only training) achieved MAE = 8.42 ± 0.69 with CCC = -0.004 ± 0.087. However, a demographic Ridge baseline using only age, sex, disease duration, height, and weight achieved MAE = 7.44 ± 0.75 (CCC = 0.326 ± 0.102), outperforming all IMU models on MAE. This finding underscores that total UPDRS-III within a PD cohort is substantially driven by disease stage (age, duration) rather than moment-to-moment motor state.

PD-only LOOCV (N = 98) confirmed this pattern: FM-augmented IMU MAE = 8.15 versus demographic baseline MAE = 7.86 (bootstrap p = 0.59, not significant). Calibration was poor for both: IMU slope = 0.256, demographic slope = 0.211 (ideal = 1.0), reflecting severe regression to the PD-group mean.

Crucially, however, a permutation test confirmed that the IMU model significantly outperforms a mean-prediction baseline (padj = 0.0021), and partial correlation controlling for age and disease duration yielded r = 0.36 (padj = 0.0021), demonstrating that inertial sensors capture motor severity information beyond what demographics encode. The severity-preserving signal exists, but it is diluted by the 12 non-directly-observable items (7 partially observable + 5 unobservable) that constitute 82% of the total score range.

HC-augmented training (PD+HC) degraded PD-only prediction substantially: MAE = 10.14 ± 0.99 versus 8.42 ± 0.69 for PD-only training (+20%). The model learns to distinguish PD from HC (scoring HC near zero) rather than discriminating within-PD severity—a qualitatively different and clinically less useful task.

Pre-registered held-out test (used once)
FM-fused StackHeld-out (N=36)9.360.5590.6150.3730.397
FM-fused Stack (PD only)Held-out (N=21 PD)9.480.2630.3190.0550.168

B1: PD-only training. B2: HC-augmented training (PD+HC train, PD-only eval). 10-split: multi-label stratified (UPDRS bins × age terciles). Held-out PD subset (N=21) is underpowered (Spearman p = 0.20).

In PD-only LOOCV, only 25.5% of predictions fell within the MCID of 3.25 points, 37.8% within 5 points, and 68.4% within 10 points. The Spearman rank correlation (ρ = 0.382, padj = 0.0008) confirms that the model preserves severity ordering despite poor absolute calibration. Weighted kappa for quartile-level agreement was 0.245 (fair). Proportional bias analysis revealed a mild slope of 0.125 (p = 0.027), and absolute prediction errors did not correlate significantly with any demographic variable (all |r| < 0.3). Sensitivity analysis for missing data showed comparable performance between subjects with complete (MAE = 8.03, N = 89) and partial recordings (MAE = 9.26, N = 9).

2.3 Three-Level Observability Decomposition

We decomposed the 18 MDS-UPDRS-III items into three tiers based on whether the assessed motor sign is physically manifest during gait with body-worn sensors (Table 3, Figure 2). Directly observable items (3.9–3.14: arising, gait, freezing, postural stability, posture, body bradykinesia) produce kinematic signatures during ambulation. Partially observable items (3.5–3.8: hand movements, pronation, toe tapping, leg agility; 3.15–3.17: tremor) produce motor signs that are indirectly reflected in gait dynamics. Not observable items (3.1–3.4: speech, facial expression, rigidity, finger tapping; 3.18: tremor constancy) require modalities that no body-worn inertial sensor can capture.

In PD-only LOOCV (N = 94, Table 3), the directly observable subscore achieved CCC = 0.56, MAE = 1.77 (r = 0.667)—the strongest concordance in this study. The partially observable tier dropped to CCC = 0.12 (MAE = 4.89), and the not-observable tier to CCC = 0.18 (MAE = 3.94). The CCC gradient—0.56 (direct) vs 0.18 (unobs) vs 0.12 (partial)—is the primary evidence for observability-driven prediction quality. Note that nMAE (MAE/max_score) does not follow this gradient (direct 7.4%, partial 7.2%, unobs 9.8%), because nMAE reflects error relative to score range, not model agreement; CCC is the appropriate metric for this comparison. The binary observable composite (items 3.7–3.14) achieved CCC = 0.46 and MAE = 3.13. Ten-split PD-only validation confirmed the pattern: direct MAE = 1.72 ± 0.33, partial MAE = 3.99 ± 0.65, not observable MAE = 3.85 ± 0.46.

TierItemsMax ScoreLOOCV MAEnMAE (%)LOOCV CCCLOOCV r10-split MAE

Not observable3.1–3.4, 3.18403.949.80.1820.2903.85 ± 0.46

LOOCV: PD-only, N=89–94 (varies by item availability). 10-split: PD-only, 10 seeds. Direct + partial + unobs = total (reconstruction error = 0.0). nMAE = MAE / max possible score × 100, enabling fair comparison across subscores with different ranges.

2.4 Item-Level Analysis and Feature–Anatomy Alignment

Per-item analysis (Figure 3) revealed a clear separation: the highest-correlation items were predominantly directly observable from gait data. Feature importance analysis showed clinically coherent sensor–item alignment: foot and trunk sensors (R_DorsalFoot, Xiphoid, LowerBack) drove direct-observable item predictions, wrist sensors drove partially observable upper-limb items, and forehead sensors contributed to not-observable items (serving as a facial expression proxy). This anatomical alignment validates that the model captures genuine biomechanical signals rather than learning spurious correlations.

2.5 Severity-Stratified Error and Calibration

The prediction error is severity-dependent (Figure 4, Table 4). Moderate-severity patients (UPDRS-III 12–35, N = 72) achieved MAE ≈ 6 (CCC = 0.07–0.15), while extreme quartiles (Q1 < 12 and Q4 > 35) had MAE > 14 with opposing biases: the model over-predicts mild patients (+14.1 points) and under-predicts severe patients (−14.3 points). Per-quartile calibration slopes ranged from 0.29 (Q2) to 0.88 (Q1), with all quartiles showing CCC < 0.16—indicating the model's overall agreement is driven by between-quartile variation rather than within-quartile discrimination. The overall calibration slope of 0.256 (ideal = 1.0) confirms severe regression to the PD-group mean (intercept = 16.3), a known phenomenon in small-N regression problems. Bland–Altman analysis revealed a bias of -1.9 points with 95% limits of agreement [-21.5, 17.7], with proportional bias (slope = -0.69, r = -0.51, p < 0.001) indicating greater underprediction at higher severity.

Table 4. Severity-stratified prediction metrics (PD-only FM LOOCV, N=98).
QuartileNMAECCCrBiasCal. slope
Q1(<12)914.090.1410.605+14.10.882
Q2(12-20)265.960.0680.138+4.50.287
Q3(20-35)465.940.1520.231-4.00.418
Q4(>35)1714.300.1380.583-14.30.510

Bias = mean(predicted − actual). The model over-predicts mild (Q1) and under-predicts severe (Q4) patients, with CCC < 0.16 across all quartiles.

2.6 Foundation Model Embeddings

In mixed PD+HC 10-fold cross-validation (N = 178), frozen MOMENT-1-base embeddings (768 dimensions, no fine-tuning) significantly improved performance: FM-fused MAE = 7.77 ± 0.44 versus handcrafted baseline MAE = 8.49 ± 0.50 (Wilcoxon p = 0.0020), winning 9 of 10 splits (Figure 5). However, in PD-only evaluation the FM advantage diminished: PD-only 10-split FM stack MAE = 8.57 ± 0.96 versus v2 baseline 8.42 ± 0.69 (p = 0.94, not significant). This suggests the FM embeddings primarily enhance PD-versus-HC discrimination rather than within-PD severity grading.

2.7 Pre-Registered Held-Out Test

On the pre-registered held-out test set (N = 36: 21 PD, 15 HC; seed = 20260309; Figure 6), the FM-augmented model achieved MAE = 9.36 (CCC = 0.559, r = 0.615), outperforming the demographic baseline (MAE = 10.35, CCC = 0.293). On the PD subset (N = 21), MAE = 9.48 with CCC = 0.263; however, with only 21 PD subjects, this comparison is underpowered (Spearman p = 0.20). The directly observable subscore on the held-out PD subset (N = 20) achieved MAE = 4.12 (CCC = 0.301), corroborating the LOOCV observability pattern on independent data. We emphasize that the primary evidence for model validity rests on the 98-subject PD-only LOOCV and 10-split evaluations; the held-out test provides confirmatory rather than definitive evidence.

2.8 Sensor Ablation

Using FM re-extraction per sensor configuration to eliminate data leakage (Table 5, Figure 7), the 5-sensor minimal set (lower back, bilateral wrists, bilateral ankles) matched the full 13-sensor configuration (MAE = 7.67 vs 7.72, p = 0.85). Even 2 wrist-worn sensors achieved competitive performance (7.80, p = 0.55). Only single lower-back was significantly worse (8.12, p = 0.014).

2.9 Cross-Dataset Context

Table 6 and Figure 8 contextualize our results. Direct comparison with Hssayeni et al. (MAE = 5.95, N = 24 PD) and Shuqair et al. (r = 0.89, same dataset) is complicated by protocol differences: cohort size (N = 98 vs 24), task type (controlled gait vs free-living ADL), sensor placement (13 full-body vs wrist+ankle), and evaluation (LOOCV on 98 vs 24). The remaining performance gap likely reflects genuine differences in signal content rather than modeling quality.

2.10 What Did Not Work

Negative results strengthen the ceiling argument. Seven end-to-end deep learning configurations across three architecture families (Transformer with MAE/contrastive/scratch pretraining, InceptionTime with MIL/ordinal/expanded, SensorGNN; Table S1) all produced MAE > 10, consistent with overfitting at N = 178. Additional approaches that failed to improve on the MAE ≈ 8 ceiling included: individual item decomposition and summation (52% worse), mixture-of-experts by severity, severity-stratified training, cross-sensor coordination features (phase-locking value, coherence, eigenvalue decomposition—all indistinguishable from noise), two-stage observable-to-total mapping, hyperparameter sweeps (Latin hypercube over 6 LightGBM parameters), and freezing-of-gait transfer (AUC = 0.500, only 14/178 subjects had FoG events). That 12+ diverse approaches converge on the same ceiling on this dataset, while the directly observable subdomain achieves CCC = 0.56, is circumstantial evidence consistent with an observability-driven barrier, though cross-dataset replication is needed to confirm generalizability.

3. Discussion

3.1 The Observability Ceiling

Our central finding is not about total UPDRS-III prediction accuracy per se, but the identification of a probable observability ceiling. The MDS-UPDRS Part III assesses 18 motor domains, but only 6 produce motor signs directly manifest during ambulation. Rigidity (item 3.3) requires passive manipulation by an examiner—no body-worn sensor can detect the resistance a clinician feels. Speech (3.1) and facial expression (3.2) are auditory and visual assessments. Upper extremity items (3.4–3.6) are administered while seated. Rest tremor (3.17–3.18) manifests during stillness, not movement.

The convergence evidence is suggestive: 12+ distinct modeling approaches—from parameter-free random kernels to 310M-parameter pretrained transformers—all converge on MAE ≈ 8 for total UPDRS-III in PD-only evaluation on this dataset. The directly observable items (CCC = 0.56) vs partially observable (CCC = 0.12) vs not observable (CCC = 0.18) gradient is consistent with what information theory predicts: a single measurement modality cannot capture what it cannot sense. We acknowledge that this conclusion rests on a single dataset with a specific item-tier assignment; cross-dataset replication with independent clinician-validated tier classifications would strengthen the claim.

3.2 The Demographic Baseline Problem

That a Ridge regression on five demographic variables (age, sex, disease duration, height, weight) achieves MAE = 7.44 on PD-only total UPDRS-III—competitive with or better than all IMU models—raises a critical question: does the IMU model measure motor severity, or has it learned to predict disease stage? Three pieces of evidence indicate real motor signal: (1) partial correlation r = 0.36 (padj = 0.002) after controlling for age and disease duration; (2) feature–anatomy alignment showing foot sensors predict gait items and wrist sensors predict upper-limb items; (3) the directly observable subscore achieves CCC = 0.56, far exceeding what demographics alone could produce for gait-specific items. The demographic competitiveness on total UPDRS-III is driven by unobservable items that correlate with disease stage but not with gait kinematics.

3.3 Clinical Implications

The directly observable subscore (items 3.9–3.14) achieves MAE = 1.77 with CCC = 0.56 in PD-only LOOCV. This has two clinical implications. First, rather than targeting total UPDRS-III—an endpoint that conflates what sensors can and cannot observe—the directly observable motor subdomain may be a more appropriate endpoint for wearable monitoring studies. Second, the sub-MCID accuracy for this subdomain suggests that between-visit tracking of gait-related motor decline is feasible with current sensor technology.

This reframes the clinical question from “Can wearables replace the neurologist?” (no) to “Can wearables provide continuous tracking of the motor functions they can actually observe?” (our data suggests yes, for gait/posture items). We note, following reviewer guidance, that the MCID of 3.25 points was derived for longitudinal change detection, not cross-sectional prediction error2; the comparison is contextual rather than definitive.

3.4 Foundation Models for Clinical Time Series

Frozen MOMENT-1 embeddings (pretrained on general time series, no clinical fine-tuning) reduced MAE from 8.49 to 7.77 (p = 0.0020) in mixed PD+HC evaluation. This “DL as feature extractor, not regressor” paradigm circumvents the overfitting that causes end-to-end deep learning to fail at N = 178. Importantly, the FM advantage diminished in PD-only evaluation (padj = 0.94), suggesting the embeddings primarily encode features useful for PD-vs-HC group separation rather than within-PD severity grading. This distinction matters for clinical deployment: a model that accurately screens (separates PD from HC) may not accurately grade (ranks severity within PD).

3.5 Limitations

Several limitations should be considered. (1) Results are from a single dataset; cross-dataset transfer validation is needed, and the observability tier classification should be independently validated by blinded movement-disorder specialists. (2) All recordings are from controlled gait tasks, not free-living conditions. (3) Medication state was not systematically controlled. (4) The HC group was older than PD (74.1 vs 67.0 years, p < 0.001), potentially conflating age-related gait changes with disease effects in mixed analyses; the FM embeddings' advantage in mixed evaluation but not in PD-only evaluation may partly reflect age-related gait features rather than disease-specific signatures. (5) N = 98 PD subjects limits statistical power, particularly for the held-out PD subset (N = 21). (6) Severe calibration (slope = 0.26) means predictions cannot be taken at face value—the model systematically compresses toward the group mean; this positions the current model as suitable for severity ranking and monitoring rather than absolute score estimation. (7) This is cross-sectional; longitudinal data are needed to validate change detection. (8) The three-level observability classification, while clinically motivated, involves judgment calls for partially observable items. (9) 23 PD subjects had deep brain stimulation (DBS), which may alter gait patterns independently of UPDRS-III score; DBS-stratified sensitivity analysis was not performed.

3.6 Future Directions

Four directions are most promising. First, cross-dataset transfer on PADS9 and other PD wearable datasets would test generalizability. Second, longitudinal within-subject tracking using the directly observable subdomain could enable treatment response monitoring where absolute accuracy matters less than change detection. Third, the sensor reduction pathway from 13 to 5 (or 2) sensors is essential for clinical adoption, though requires independent validation with the foundation model pipeline. Fourth, addressing the severe calibration bias (slope = 0.26) through severity-weighted loss functions, synthetic oversampling for continuous targets (SMOGN), or personalized within-subject baselines could substantially improve clinical utility at severity extremes.

4. Methods

4.1 Dataset

WearGait-PD7 (Synapse syn55052683) comprises 100 PD and 85 HC participants, of whom 178 (98 PD, 80 HC) had complete recordings. Each subject wore 13 Xsens MTw Awinda IMU sensors placed at: lower back, bilateral wrists, bilateral mid-lateral thighs, bilateral lateral shanks, bilateral dorsal feet, bilateral ankles, xiphoid process, and forehead. Sensors sampled at 100 Hz recording triaxial accelerometer and gyroscope data (78 total channels). Participants completed six standardized tasks: self-paced walking, hurried-pace walking, Timed Up-and-Go, balance assessment, and tandem gait, plus pressure-mat variants. Motor severity was assessed using MDS-UPDRS Part III by trained clinicians.

4.2 Evaluation Protocol

Three complementary evaluation protocols were used. PD-only LOOCV (N = 98): the primary evaluation, most comparable to published work, leave-one-subject-out with all metrics computed on the left-out PD patient. PD-only 10-split CV (N = 98): multi-label stratified splits (UPDRS bins × age terciles) with 10 independent seeds, providing variance estimates. Pre-registered held-out test (N = 36: 21 PD, 15 HC; seed = 20260309): the test set was specified before any model selection and used exactly once. All splits maintained strict subject-level separation.

4.3 Feature Extraction

Recordings were segmented into 10-second windows (1,000 samples) with 50% overlap. Handcrafted features (1,752): Per sensor and channel: RMS, standard deviation, range, IQR, skewness, kurtosis, jerk, zero-crossing rate; Welch PSD in locomotor (0.5–3 Hz), tremor (3–8 Hz), and high-frequency (8–25 Hz) bands with band ratios; spectral entropy; autocorrelation-based gait regularity. Additional features: foot contact spatiotemporal metrics, balance sway, task-contrast deltas, walkway distillation, and clinical covariates (age, sex, height, weight, years since diagnosis, DBS status). Foundation model embeddings (768): Frozen MOMENT-1-base encoder8 (v0.1, Hugging Face AutonLab/MOMENT-1-base), 768-dimensional embeddings from 26 accelerometer/gyroscope magnitude channels, truncated to 512 samples (5.12 s at 100 Hz), per-channel z-normalized (zero mean, unit variance within each recording), averaged across all recordings per subject to yield one embedding vector per subject. Embeddings are deterministic (no gradient computation, no dropout).

4.4 Feature Selection and Model

XGBoost gain-based importance ranking (n_estimators=300, max_depth=4, lr=0.05, reg_lambda=2.0) selected K = 150 features (handcrafted-only) or K = 300 (fused). Selection was performed within each training fold. The primary model was LightGBM (n_estimators=2,000, lr=0.03, max_depth=6, reg_lambda=3.0, MAE objective, early stopping patience=100 on 15% validation). Multi-seed ensemble averaged predictions across seeds [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]. Demographic baseline: Ridge regression (α = 1.0) on age, sex, disease duration, height, weight. Ten-split cross-validation seeds: [0–9]. Held-out test split: seed = 20260309 (specified before any model development on this split).

4.5 Three-Level Observability Classification

Items were classified based on whether the motor sign is physically manifest during gait with body-worn sensors. Directly observable (3.9–3.14): arising, gait, freezing, postural stability, posture, body bradykinesia—all produce measurable kinematic signals during ambulation. Partially observable (3.5–3.8, 3.15–3.17): hand movements, pronation-supination, toe tapping, leg agility, postural/kinetic/rest tremor. These items are classified as partially (rather than directly) observable because the MDS-UPDRS-III administration protocol requires them to be performed as isolated, rapid, repetitive movements while seated—a biomechanically distinct regime from the cyclic locomotor pattern of gait. For example, toe tapping (3.7) and leg agility (3.8) engage overlapping lower-limb musculature but test rapid alternating movement speed and amplitude decrement, not the bilateral pendular coordination captured by gait accelerometry. Tremor items (3.15–3.17) may be reflected in wrist or ankle sensor noise during ambulation, but the signal is attenuated by movement artifact. Not observable (3.1–3.4, 3.18): speech, facial expression, rigidity, finger tapping, tremor constancy—require modalities beyond inertial sensing.

4.6 Statistical Analysis

Primary agreement metric: Lin's concordance correlation coefficient (CCC), which measures both correlation and calibration. BCa bootstrap CIs (N = 10,000) stratified by PD/HC. Model comparisons: subject-level paired bootstrap for LOOCV, Wilcoxon signed-rank for 10-split. Multiple comparison correction: Holm–Bonferroni across 8 primary tests. Effect sizes: Cohen's d. Clinical context: MCID = 3.25 (Horvath 2015)2, applied as a contextual benchmark rather than a formal threshold. Bland–Altman for systematic bias. Partial correlation controlling for age and disease duration.

4.7 Code and Data Availability

WearGait-PD is available on Synapse (syn55052683)7. Analysis code will be available at [repository URL].

References

  1. GBD 2019 Collaborators. Global, regional, and national burden of neurological disorders, 1990–2019. Lancet Neurol. 20, 797–820 (2021).
  2. Horvath, K. et al. Minimal clinically important difference on the Motor Examination part of MDS-UPDRS. Parkinsonism Relat. Disord. 21, 1421–1426 (2015).
  3. Hssayeni, M. D. et al. Wearable sensors for estimation of Parkinsonian tremor severity during free body movement. BioMed. Eng. OnLine 20, 24 (2021).
  4. Shuqair, H. et al. Self-supervised representation learning for motor severity estimation. Bioengineering 11, 689 (2024).
  5. Sotirakis, C. et al. Identification of motor progression in Parkinson's disease using wearable sensors. npj Parkinsons Dis. 9, 74 (2023).
  6. Li, J. et al. TRIP: Transformer-based IMU pretraining for Parkinson's disease. arXiv 2510.15748 (2025).
  7. WearGait-PD dataset. Sci. Data (2026). doi:10.1038/s41597-026-06806-2.
  8. Goswami, M. et al. MOMENT: A family of open time-series foundation models. ICML (2024).
  9. Varghese, J. et al. PADS: Parkinson's disease smartwatch dataset. PhysioNet (2024).

Testp (raw)p (adjusted)Significance
P1_fm_vs_v2_median0.46920.9384ns
P1_fm_vs_demo_median0.29910.8973ns

Three tests survive correction: permutation (FM > mean), Spearman severity ranking, and partial correlation (IMU signal beyond demographics).
